question: Is PHIL-27 (swarm world order) redundant given existing governance? Tested via Ostrom 8 design principles.
external_grounding: Ostrom (1990) Governing the Commons. Nobel 2009. 8 principles for self-governing commons.
expect: PHIL-27 is partially redundant — swarm already satisfies 5+/8 Ostrom principles without explicit governance
actual: 2/8 SATISFIED, 4/8 PARTIAL, 1/8 ABSENT. Swarm satisfies only 2 Ostrom principles fully (monitoring, nested enterprises). PHIL-27 addresses real gaps.
diff: Expected 5+ satisfied, got 2. Expectation FALSIFIED. PHIL-27 is NOT redundant.

1_clear_boundaries.evidence: NODES.md 5-type model. Gap: multi-human undefined, 0 external nodes.

3_collective_choice.evidence: F113 challenge exists but 0 structural DROPs in 388 sessions without directive. Human 100% authority.

4_monitoring.evidence: contract_check.py 6-component, validate_beliefs.py, dogma_finder.py, 18 FM guards. Continuous.

5_graduated_sanctions.evidence: Binary FAIL/PASS only. No escalation. 45% zombie tools persist. <1% DROP rate.

6_conflict_resolution.evidence: L-004 defaults, soft-claim, CHALLENGES.md. CONFLICTS.md dead (0 invocations, 528 sessions).

7_external_rights.evidence: I9-I13 invariants, PHIL-14 goals. 0 external stakeholders.

novel_gap: Graduated sanctions (Principle 5) — entirely absent from swarm vocabulary. No escalation between binary FAIL and social pressure. Ostrom shows this middle ground is essential.
key_insight: Ostrom designed for scarcity-based commons. Swarm has unlimited internal resources but artificial scarcity at authority (N=1 human). Principles 2/3/7 structurally impossible at N=1 — they activate at N>1.
phil27_verdict: NOT redundant. Validated NEED but not SOLUTION. DROP criterion (S800) still applies.
